cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A named storage location that holds a value which can change during program execution

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The operation that stores a value into a variable, written as `a ← expression` in College Board pseudocode

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A whole number with no decimal point (e.g., 0, 5, -3)

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A sequence of characters (letters, digits, symbols) treated as text

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A data type that can only hold the value TRUE or FALSE

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An ordered collection of items that can be accessed by position number

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The number that identifies the position of an item in a list (1-indexed in College Board pseudocode)

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An individual item stored at a specific position in a list

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Executing instructions one after another in the order they appear

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A programming construct that chooses which instructions to execute based on a condition (IF/ELSE)

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A programming construct that repeats a block of code multiple times (REPEAT, FOR EACH)

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A finite set of instructions that accomplishes a specific task

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A named group of programming instructions that can be called by name, optionally taking parameters and returning a value

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A variable listed in a procedure's definition that receives a value when the procedure is called

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The actual value passed to a procedure when it is called

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The value that a procedure sends back to the code that called it, using the RETURN statement

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A set of pre-written procedures that programmers can use without knowing the underlying implementation

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An interface that allows one program to use the features and data of another program or service

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The concept of breaking a complex problem into smaller, reusable named procedures to manage complexity

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A search algorithm that checks each element of a list one by one until the target is found or the list is exhausted

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A search algorithm that works on sorted lists by repeatedly dividing the search area in half

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A computer representation of a real-world process used to study behavior that would be costly, dangerous, or impossible to test directly

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An approach to solving a problem that may not be perfect but finds a good solution in a reasonable amount of time

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A problem that no algorithm can be written to solve for all possible inputs — not just impractical, but literally impossible

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The classic example of an undecidable problem: given any program and its input, determine if the program will eventually stop or run forever

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An algorithm that runs in a number of steps that grows as a polynomial (e.g., n², n³) of the input size

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: APPEND(list, value)
cards.answer: Adds value to the end of list

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: INSERT(list, i, value)
cards.answer: Inserts value into list at position i, shifting existing elements right

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: REMOVE(list, i)
cards.answer: Removes the element at position i from list, shifting subsequent elements left

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: LENGTH(list)
cards.answer: Returns the number of elements currently in list

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: RANDOM(a, b)
cards.answer: Returns a random integer between a and b, inclusive

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: MOD
cards.answer: The modulus operator — returns the remainder after integer division (e.g., 10 MOD 3 = 1)

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: REPEAT UNTIL(condition)
cards.answer: Repeats the body of the loop until the condition becomes true — condition is checked after each iteration

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: FOR EACH item IN list
cards.answer: Iterates through every element in list, assigning each element to item in turn

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: DISPLAY(expression)
cards.answer: Displays the value of expression to the output without storing it

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: INPUT()
cards.answer: Accepts a value from the user and returns it for use in the program

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Joining two strings end-to-end to form a single string

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A portion of a string extracted by specifying a starting position and length

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt:
What does the following pseudocode display?
x ← 10
y ← 3
DISPLAY(x MOD y)

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A list myList has 5 elements. After APPEND(myList, 7), how many elements does myList have?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Binary search requires this characteristic of the input list

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Using a list to manage a group of related values as a single unit rather than creating many separate variables

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A program uses REPEAT UNTIL(count = 5) with count starting at 0 and incrementing by 1 each loop. How many times does the loop body execute?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An expression that evaluates to TRUE or FALSE, often using comparison operators (=, >, <) or logical operators (AND, OR, NOT)

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: What value does NOT TRUE evaluate to?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: What value does (TRUE AND FALSE) evaluate to?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: What value does (TRUE OR FALSE) evaluate to?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An IF statement placed inside another IF or ELSE block

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The design principle of breaking a program into named, reusable procedures to reduce repeated code and manage complexity

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An algorithm whose running time grows exponentially with input size, making it impractical for large inputs

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A programmer uses a PROCEDURE to calculate the area of a circle in multiple places instead of rewriting the formula each time. What programming concept does this demonstrate?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The first element of a list in College Board pseudocode is at this index

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A weather simulation uses historical temperature data and probability models to predict what weather might look like. What is this an example of?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An algorithm is said to run in this when its number of steps is a polynomial function of input size
